instruction:
Considering the issue of electron emission in vacuum tubes, let the number of electrons arriving at the anode per unit time, denoted as $N$, follow a Poisson distribution $$P\{N=k\}=\frac{\lambda^{k}}{k!}\mathrm{e}^{-\lambda}.$$ The energy carried by each electron forms a sequence of random variables $X_1, X_2, \cdots, X_k, \cdots$. It is known that $\{X_k\}$ is statistically independent of $N$, and $\{X_k\}$ are uncorrelated with each other, all having the same mean and variance $$E\langle X_k\rangle=\eta,\quad D(X_k)=\sigma^2.$$ The energy received by the anode per unit time is $S=\sum_{k=1}^{N}X_k$. Find variance $D(S)$ of $S$ is ().

Options:
A. $$ \begin{aligned} {{D ( S )}} & {{} {{}=\lambda \cdot \sigma}} \\ \end{aligned} $$
B. $$ \begin{aligned} {{D ( S )}} & {{} {{}=\lambda^{2} \cdot \sigma}} \\ \end{aligned} $$
C. $$
\begin{aligned} {{D ( S )}} & {{} {{}=\lambda( \sigma^{2}+\eta^{2} )}} \\ \end{aligned}
$$
D. $$ \begin{aligned} {{D ( S )}} & {{} {{}=\sigma \cdot \eta^{2}}} \\ \end{aligned} $$
E. $$ \begin{aligned} {{D ( S )}} & {{} {{}=\eta^{2} \cdot \lambda}} \\ \end{aligned} $$
F. $$ \begin{aligned} {{D ( S )}} & {{} {{}=\sigma^{2}+\eta}} \\ \end{aligned} $$
G. $$ \begin{aligned} {{D ( S )}} & {{} {{}=\lambda \cdot \sigma^{2}}} \\ \end{aligned} $$
H. $$ \begin{aligned} {{D ( S )}} & {{} {{}=\lambda + \eta^{2}}} \\ \end{aligned} $$
I. $$ \begin{aligned} {{D ( S )}} & {{} {{}=\lambda \cdot \eta}} \\ \end{aligned} $$
J. $$ \begin{aligned} {{D ( S )}} & {{} {{}=\sigma^{2} + \eta^{2}}} \\ \end{aligned} $$
